content.problemStatement:
The way you counted the number of paths in the previous problem is called the ***Rule of Sum***. 

Formally, if there are $A$ ways of doing a task and $B$ ways of doing another task and both are dependant on each other - you can either perform the first task or the second task then there are $A$ $+$ $B$ total ways.

This can be extended to more than 2 tasks. \
In general if there are $N$ tasks and there are $A_i$ ways of doing the $i$-th task and you can perform exactly one task at a time, then there are $\sum_{i=1}^N A_i$ total number of ways.

**Note:** Sigma notation is a method used to express a long sum in a concise way. 

$\sum_{i=1}^NA_i$ = $A_1$ + $A_2$ + $A_3$ ..... + $A_N$
constraints:
- $1 \leq T \leq 1000$
- $2 \leq N \leq 10^5$
- $1 \leq A_i \leq 10^9$
- The sum of $N$ across all test cases does not exceed $10^5$
content.inputFormat:
- The first line of input will contain a single integer $T$, denoting the number of test cases. The description of the test cases follows.
- The first line of each test case contains a single integer $N$, denoting the length of array $A$.
- The second line of each test case contains $N$ space-separated integers $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N$ — denoting the array $A$.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a new line the maximum value of $A_1+A_N$ you can get after several right rotations.
